excerpt: The Qur'an's literary inimitability (iʿjāz) remained an unmet challenge across history
source_location_hint: On the impossibility of producing a surah equal to the Qur'an

excerpt: The successor receives comprehensive knowledge (ʿilm) from the Prophet, with each gate of knowledge disclosing further gates
source_location_hint: On the legatee's knowledge and spiritual authority

excerpt: The Companions of ʿAlī—Salmān, Miqdād, Abū Dharr, ʿAmmār—exemplify the standards of witnessing to truth
source_location_hint: On the reliable witnesses to the Prophet's designations

excerpt: Jinn and humans alone are capable of disobedience; angels are bound to obey God's command
source_location_hint: On the natures of jinn, humans, and angels

excerpt: Truth (ḥaqq) is that which endures unchanging; falsehood (bāṭil) perpetually shifts and dissolves
source_location_hint: On the nature of truth and falsehood in relation to the Hereafter

excerpt: The heart contains truth untainted; falsehood emerges when that truth is clothed in language
source_location_hint: On the distinction between inner truth and external utterance

excerpt: The connective particle 'wa' (and) unites only things similar and corresponding in nature or relation
source_location_hint: On the logic of Qur'anic syntax and conjunction

excerpt: Refusal to acknowledge the Imāms despite witnessing their virtue constitutes a species of disbelief harder than stone
source_location_hint: On the hardness of heart toward the Imāms

excerpt: Three groups fall victim to the fire of prophethood: explicit disbelievers, depreciators of the Imāms, and exaggerators of their status
source_location_hint: On the varieties of enmity toward the Prophet's legacy

excerpt: Īmān (faith) requires both affirmation and security—the believer affirms God while God grants security from destruction
source_location_hint: On the dual meaning of faith

excerpt: God, the angels, and the righteous believers bear witness to God's unity and justice
source_location_hint: On the witnesses to divine unity

excerpt: One who enters the Imām's summons with purified intention remains in prayer so long as he does not nullify his oath through mockery or opposition
source_location_hint: On the condition of the believer within the Imām's community

excerpt: The saints' inner service continues uninterrupted though their outward stations are usurped; they remain alive in God's sight
source_location_hint: On the eternal life of the Imāms and saints

excerpt: The elevated scholar (in rabwah) stands closer to those above in receiving knowledge and transmits blessings to those below
source_location_hint: On the scholar's rank and function

excerpt: The garden receiving heavy rain produces twice its yield; the Imām's knowledge makes the scholar doubly fruitful in guiding others
source_location_hint: On the scholar's reception of the Imām's knowledge

excerpt: To affirm (āmana) means to confirm, and confirmation requires understanding and truthfulness on the part of the affirmer. A person cannot genuinely confirm what he does not comprehend.
source_location_hint: Commentary on Qur'an 3:7

excerpt: Those endowed with knowledge witness to truth; they stand upon justice. Not every scholar can be placed among the ʿulamāʾ ʿalā ḥaqq, yet every such knower is indeed a scholar.
source_location_hint: Discourse on the Rasikhun fi al-ilm

excerpt: Allah has established His religion upon the pattern of His creation, that He might be judged by creation and His religion made one.
source_location_hint: Commentary on Qur'an 41:53

excerpt: The things of this world possess no motion save through an inner stimulus. Within all creation lie intrinsic meanings, invisible yet intelligible to the discerning.
source_location_hint: On divine wisdom and creation

excerpt: After contemplating these arguments, who but the stubbornly obstinate would deny the reality of esoteric exegesis and its knowledge?
source_location_hint: Sermon on taʾwīl

excerpt: Mecca is the city; Bakkah is its essence. In deeper reading: Mecca is Muhammad; Bakkah is ʿAlī—the very point upon which the House is erected.
source_location_hint: Commentary on Qur'an 3:96

excerpt: All the prophets made pilgrimage to the same Ka'ba—a sign confirming its primacy and the necessity of all believers accepting the Imamite authority.
source_location_hint: On the Ka'ba's universal significance

excerpt: ʿAlī's station is immutable; all believers must turn toward him, advancing in mystical understanding and devotion, just as all earthly bodies move toward the unmoved Ka'ba.
source_location_hint: On spiritual hierarchy and walāya

excerpt: The Anwār (Lights) are gathered on Friday and dispersed on Saturday. The Prophet gathered all Anwār in ʿAlī; ʿAlī broadcast them to all the Imāms.
source_location_hint: On the emanation of divine lights through the Imams

excerpt: Bayt (house) signifies the home—essentially, the Imām's very essence—wherein the souls of all believers find eternal rest.
source_location_hint: Commentary on the House of Allah

excerpt: The word 'blessing' in this verse refers to the chain of Imāms descending from ʿAlī and Fāṭima until the Day of Judgment.
source_location_hint: On the continuation of Imamite succession

excerpt: The hadīth sharīf (noble tradition) pleases people alone; the hadīth qudsī (sacred tradition) pleases both Allah and humanity.
source_location_hint: On prophetic and sacred utterances

excerpt: There exists a hadīth qudsī in which the Prophet declared: 'The example of ʿAlī is the example of the Ka'ba.' Whatever is thought of the Ka'ba shall be thought of ʿAlī.
source_location_hint: On the spiritual equivalence of ʿAlī and the Ka'ba

excerpt: ʿAlī declared: 'I am the point of the world'—meaning: 'No matter how elevated I am in this physical realm, I remain subordinate in rank to the Messenger of Allah!'
source_location_hint: Sermon on humility and the Prophet's supremacy

excerpt: When the Qur'ān is abandoned, even apparent knowledge slips from the hands of the apparent worshippers, leaving them confused in ignorance.
source_location_hint: On the consequences of abandoning divine guidance

excerpt: Every servant of the summons is called a Lord (Rabb), yet by 'Lord' we mean those called to the inner world—the Imāms—sent forth by Allah Himself.
source_location_hint: On divine titles and Imamate

excerpt: Even the prayer of a worshipper lacking inner attachment to walāya is rendered useless.
source_location_hint: On the necessity of walāya for spiritual perfection

excerpt: The infallible Imāms stand in the position of the soul to a corpse. When they are removed from their station, the path of guidance is destroyed.
source_location_hint: On the Imams as the inner verses of the Qur'ān

excerpt: ʿAmīr al-Muʾminīn, expressing his excellence, declared: 'I am a clear sign!' employing the plural form because all Imāms who are signs descend from him.
source_location_hint: On ʿAlī as the sign of Allah

excerpt: Words and phrases are lifeless; what sense could arrogance bear against the inanimate? The verses must refer to living, conscious beings—the Imāms.
source_location_hint: On why the verses denote the Imams, not scriptural text

excerpt: The invitation of the Family of Muhammad is a potential paradise leading to the actual paradise.
source_location_hint: On the two paradises: potential and actual

excerpt: One who believes in the outer invitation of the Prophet must enter the inner invitation of ʿAlī, die while attached to his walāya and the walāya of his descendants, to be reborn in the Hereafter and enter Paradise.
source_location_hint: On the necessity of ʿAlī's walāya for salvation

excerpt: The Prophet said: 'One should not pray when a camel stands before one, for every camel harbors a devil'—this hadīth, understood through wisdom, reveals a profound truth.
source_location_hint: Hadīth on the camel and the arrogant soul

excerpt: The walī Allāh (guardian of Allah) is he who prepares the garment of piety, making its wearer a miracle in Allah's sight.
source_location_hint: On the walī's role in spiritual perfection

excerpt: Only when they sit humbly—no matter how despised they deem the walī—can they grasp these sciences and don the garment of piety.
source_location_hint: On humility as the gate to gnosis

excerpt: The king acquiesced. He descended and seated himself before the preacher with respect. The preacher said: 'You are the camel by virtue of your power. This covenant is the needle's eye through which you have passed by accepting your humiliation.'
source_location_hint: Story of the King and the Preacher, 22nd Assembly

excerpt: *(This entire section from Majālis al-Muʾayyada, 22nd Assembly)*
source_location_hint: Complete sermon on Qur'an 7:40

excerpt: Three verbs relating to sight, yet each bearing a distinct esoteric meaning: Tara (seeing with the eye of direct knowledge and divine illumination), Yanzur (looking with the physical eye), Basr (perceiving with the eye of reason and inner understanding).
source_location_hint: Commentary on Qur'an 7:198

excerpt: When the common scholars could not furnish a proper interpretation, they resorted to saying that these are matters of Allah's dominion requiring no intellect.
source_location_hint: On the misuse of taslīm (submission) in place of understanding

excerpt: Had people instead resorted to the heirs of the Prophet's knowledge—those who uphold the Book and the Balance of Justice—Allah would surely have illumined their beliefs and resolved all difficulties.
source_location_hint: On the necessity of the Imams as guarantors of knowledge

excerpt: Verily, Our Word unto a thing when We intend it is only that We say unto it: 'Be,' and it is.
source_location_hint: Qur'an 16:40

excerpt: In its state of potentiality, it is ratq (joined, undifferentiated); when made verbal (actual), it is faqq (separated, formed, distinct). What is the essence by which non-existence becomes existence but the divine Command?
source_location_hint: On the dialectic of potentiality and actuality in creation

excerpt: Just as the outer heavens and earth produce only flesh, blood, and bone—all destined for decay—the inner heavens and earth grant eternal life and immortal survival to souls.
source_location_hint: On the inner and outer orders of creation

excerpt: Everything whose existence is eternal and fixed is the same and that is God... this religion is perfect, it is not perishable, and it shall endure from everlasting to everlasting
source_location_hint: Early Fatimid exegesis on divine unity and the eternal nature of religion

excerpt: Just as the face of something gives us the knowledge of the true reality of that thing, in the same way, these saints of Allah give us the true knowledge of the Oneness of Allah
source_location_hint: On the function of the Imams as guides to divine knowledge

excerpt: He who has attained the knowledge of his soul has attained the knowledge of his Lord
source_work: Hadith tradition cited in Ismaili texts
source_location_hint: Spiritual psychology in Ismaili thought

excerpt: The religion of Mateen consists of two acts of worship. One is scholarly worship and the other is practical worship... the body has been made obligatory to worship the practical worship which dominates the body
source_location_hint: On the dual nature of devotion: knowledge and action

excerpt: Orbit refers to the shape of a circle. No planet can get out of its sky. In the same way, every stage of the da'wah has a circle in which he performs his duties
source_location_hint: On the hierarchical organization of the divine summons

excerpt: Anna wa Ali min Noor Wahid (Ali and I are from the same light)... From Our light came the earth and the heavens, then from our light came the light of beauty and beauty
source_location_hint: On the cosmic significance of the five pure ones

excerpt: Adorn your gatherings with the remembrance of Ali ibn Abi Talib, for the remembrance of Ali is my remembrance, and my remembrance is the remembrance of Allah, and the remembrance of Allah is worship
source_authority: Sacred tradition preserved in Ismaili sources
source_location_hint: On devotion through remembrance of the Imam

excerpt: The Prophet saw the future with his foresight and divine support, in which he saw Ali being martyred, the door falling on Fatima, Hasan drinking poison and Husayn beheaded
source_location_hint: On the prophetic foreknowledge of the Ahl al-Bayt's trials

excerpt: Today We have perfected the religion for you and have chosen Islam as the best day for you... at that time, the work of his prophethood was completed and he asked for his reward
source_authority: Interpretation of Quran 5:3 in relation to Ghadir
source_location_hint: On the completion of the din and the request for love of Ahl al-Bayt

excerpt: The true meaning of this verse is that the Ummah will benefit only the Ummah from the reward... Neither the Prophet nor the Ahl al-Bayt will benefit... Allah is the only one who rewards the Messenger of Allah and the Ahl al-Bayt
source_location_hint: On the beneficiaries of the requested recompense

excerpt: The reward of the Prophethood is the love of the Imams of the Wilayat of Amir al-Mu'minin... only those who perform this reward... enter their hearts with faith
source_location_hint: On the necessity of love for the Imams

excerpt: His prior sin was an inclination to place Abu Bakr within the ranks of the inner da'wah... His final sin was the delay in announcing the testament concerning Ali... yet this delay in doing so was a sin in the sight of Allah. In this verse, Allah forgives the veil of Muhammad for these two transgressions
source_location_hint: On the spiritual significance of prophethood's completion

excerpt: Man is My secret and I am his secret... Understand these three dimensions of the self... the soul is closer to human consciousness than even Allah! And once you have known them, you shall declare in humility, I have not fulfilled the right of knowing You, understanding truly that the inability to perceive is itself the highest knowledge
source_location_hint: On mystical knowledge of the self and the divine

excerpt: touching (*al-mass*) here does not signify outward, physical contact but rather an inward, spiritual apprehension—the grasping of meaning through esoteric exegesis
source_authority: Dāʿī and theologian of the Fatimid Ismaili community
source_location_hint: assembly discussions on Quranic interpretation

excerpt: dhālik (that/distant) versus hādhā (this/near) — the opening of the Qur'an employs the distant form to invite contemplation of hidden meanings
source_location_hint: discussions on the Qur'an's external and internal dimensions

excerpt: apparent contradictions in the Qur'an concerning divine vision and other theological matters are resolved through taʾwīl (esoteric exegesis)
source_location_hint: foundational principles of esoteric interpretation

excerpt: a preacher lifted the Qur'an and exclaimed: By Allah, this book has led nations astray—reflecting the apparent contradictions in the letter without understanding the spirit
source_authority: Dāʿī of the Fatimid Ismaili community
source_location_hint: assemblies addressing Quranic interpretation

excerpt: The Ahl al-Bayt are the soul and meaning of the Qur'an; ask the people of remembrance (ahl al-dhikr) if you do not know
source_location_hint: exegesis of Qur'an 21:7 on the Ahl al-Bayt as interpreters

excerpt: All things are preserved in the Clear Imam (al-Imam al-Mubin), paralleling the statement that all things are in the Book
source_authority: Ismaili philosopher of the 4th century AH
source_location_hint: metaphysical correspondence between Book and Imam

excerpt: The verse of purification (Ayat al-Tathhir) specifies the Ahl al-Bayt as those from whom Allah has removed uncleanness and whom He purifies utterly
source_location_hint: commentary on Qur'an 33:33 and the station of the Ahl al-Bayt

excerpt: Your Lord will give you and you will be well-pleased — Allah's bestowal upon the Prophet never ceases because his satisfaction is never final
source_location_hint: discourse on the Prophet's rank and divine grace

excerpt: Allah and His angels send blessings upon the Prophet, establishing an eternal chain of blessing that never ceases
source_location_hint: assemblies on the Prophet's virtues and the unceasing divine mercy

excerpt: The perpetual giving of Allah to the Messenger exemplifies a principle of divine grace: cessation of gift depends upon cessation of the recipient's desire
source_location_hint: lessons on divine favour and its characteristics

excerpt: Those who drank before the prohibition of intoxicants—when it was not yet forbidden—were forgiven because they were believers and doers of good
source_location_hint: assembly discussion on divine mercy and the conditions of forgiveness

excerpt: The Hereafter is better for you than the First — referring both literally to the Prophet's wives and esoterically to the Imams of the final cycle surpassing those of earlier cycles
source_location_hint: interpretation of Sura Adha on multiple levels of meaning

excerpt: The seven Aql (intellects) come under the obedience of the final configuration, and the Mim of the Hereafter surpasses the Mim of the First through the glory of the Qaim
source_location_hint: esoteric numerology and the hierarchy of cycles

excerpt: After this seventy, the era of revelation that begins shall be possessed by the Qaim Ali—the Remembrance of Peace—in whom your sovereignty continues through your son
source_location_hint: discourse on the final age and the Qaim's role

excerpt: I am the city of knowledge and Ali is its gate; whoever seeks knowledge must come through the gate — the Prophet establishing the spiritual gatekeeping of Ali
source_location_hint: sayings of the Prophet on Ali's station

excerpt: The Gate of Humility (Baab al-Hittah) in the time of Moses contrasts with the Gate of Ali in the time of Muhammad, both requiring entry through the proper door and proper intention
source_location_hint: esoteric interpretation of gates and gateways in Quranic narrative

excerpt: True piety (birr) and God-consciousness (taqwa) consist in entering the house of the Shariah through the wilayat of Ali, not through rejection or alternative allegiance
source_location_hint: exposition of piety and loyalty to the Imam's guardianship

excerpt: Allah's exhaustion or rest upon creation cannot be literal, as He is neither fatigued nor does He sleep—the six days refer to spiritual principles not temporal measures
source_location_hint: metaphysical interpretation of creation narrative
